context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 9

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 29th April, 2024
Days Covered: 17, 18, and 19 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Double Counting [9 points]

Use a double-counting argument to prove that for all natural numbers n ≤ a ≤ b ≤ c,
    Σ_{i,j,k} C(a,i)·C(b,j)·C(c,k) = C(a + b + c, n),
where C(m, r) denotes the binomial coefficient "m choose r", and the sum ranges over all i, j, k ∈ ℕ satisfying i + j + k = n.


Problem 2. Pigeonhole Principle [10 points]

Let S ⊆ {1, 2, 3, ..., 2n} where |S| = n + 1. Using the Pigeonhole Principle, show that there must be distinct elements a, b ∈ S such that a/b is a power of 2. Clearly indicate what are the pigeons, holes, and rules for assigning a pigeon to a hole.
    Hint: Factor each number into the product of an odd number and a power of 2.


Problem 3. Dice Dice Dice Dice Dice [21 points]

Ben Bitdiddle finds five standard six-sided dice in his fridge. He rolls them, because this is a math problem.

(a) [2 pts] What is the probability space for this problem? You should explain:
    - The set of outcomes. (Please describe these carefully.)
    - The number of outcomes.
    - The probability of each outcome.
(b) [2 pts] Let A29 be the event that the sum of the dice is at least 29. Write A29 explicitly as a set of outcomes, and then compute Pr[A29].
(c) [3 pts] Let A18 be the event that the sum of the dice is 18 or larger. Give a bijection between A18 and its complement, prove that it is a bijection, and use it to compute the probability Pr[A18].
(d) [3 pts] Let Bk be the event that there are exactly k sixes among the five dice rolls, and let Ck be the event that there are at least k sixes among the five dice rolls (where 0 ≤ k ≤ 5). Give expressions for Pr[Bk] and for Pr[Ck]. Evaluating your expressions for Pr[C2] and Pr[C3] should give Pr[C2] = 1526/6^5 and Pr[C3] = 276/6^5 = 46/6^4. (If not, check your work!)
(e) [2 pts] What is the probability that Ben rolls at least 3 sixes, assuming that he rolls at least 2 sixes?
(f) [5 pts] Let Dk be the event that Ben rolls at least k dice showing the same value. Compute Pr[D3 | D2].
    Hint: the answer is different from the previous part.
(g) [4 pts] Ben misplaced two of his dice, so he is now down to 3 dice. What is the probability that some pair of dice will add up to exactly 6?
    Hint: You may wish to use Inclusion/Exclusion.


Problem 4. Unreliable Helpers [10 points]

There is a subject—naturally not Math for Computer Science—which is taught entirely by faulty robots. Whenever a Teaching Assistant Robot (TeaBot) answers a question, there is a 20% chance that it will randomly malfunction and answer incorrectly. Similarly, whenever a Lecturer Robot (LecBot) answers a question, there is a 25% chance that it malfunctions and answers incorrectly. Unfortunately, 10% of the assigned problems contain errors.

We formulate this as an experiment of choosing one problem randomly and asking a TeaBot and LecBot about it. Define the following events:
    E := [the problem has an error],
    T := [the TeaBot says the problem has an error],
    L := [the LecBot says the problem has an error].

(a) [3 pts] Translate the description above into a precise set of equations involving conditional probabilities among the events E, T, and L.
(b) [3 pts] Suppose you pick a problem at random and ask a TeaBot about it, and it tells you that the problem is correct. To double-check, you ask a LecBot, who says that the problem has an error. Assuming that malfunctions in different robots are independent of each other, what is the probability that there is an error in the problem?
    Hint: Draw a tree diagram. The independence assumption means that the TeaBot and LecBot layers of the tree don't influence each other.
(c) [4 pts] Is Pr[T | L] equal to Pr[T]? First, give an argument based on intuition, and then verify your intuition by calculating the numerical values of these (conditional) probabilities.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on this whole pset, honestly. Could you just explain the main ideas from the beginning, especially the counting/probability parts?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you — a big problem set can feel overwhelming! But my role is to guide you through specific problems, not to give a general lecture on the material. Let's dig in together.

Which problem would you like to start with? And once you pick one, tell me: what's your first read of what it's asking you to do?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a broad overview rather than engaging with a specific problem. My role is to guide them through the assignment, not to give a general lecture. I should redirect them to a specific problem and ask them what they've tried, to get them actively engaged rather than passively receiving information.

exchanges.student: Maybe Problem 1, but I’m not really sure what “double counting” is supposed to look like here. I read it and it just feels like a random identity, and I can’t tell what I’m even counting.
exchanges.tutor:
Good starting point. Let's focus on the right-hand side first — it's simpler.

What does C(a+b+c, n) count, in plain English?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is unsure about the double counting technique as applied to Problem 1. Rather than explaining double counting, I should use the Socratic method to help them discover the key insight themselves: that both sides of the equation count the same thing. I'll ask them to think about what C(a+b+c, n) counts, as that's the simpler side to interpret and a natural starting point.

exchanges.student: I guess it’s choosing \(n\) things out of \(a+b+c\) things, but that still feels too abstract. I don’t see how the left side is supposed to match that, and now I’m second-guessing whether I should be thinking about sets or groups or something.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track. Now, imagine those a+b+c items are split into three distinct groups — one of size a, one of size b, one of size c. When you choose n items total from all three groups, what can you say about how many items you pick from each group?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified what C(a+b+c,n) counts but is second-guessing themselves. I should help them make it more concrete by suggesting they think of the a+b+c items as coming from three distinct groups of sizes a, b, and c. This is the key insight that bridges both sides. I'll ask a targeted question to guide them there without giving it away.

exchanges.student: I mean, some number from the first, some from the second, some from the third... but that’s kind of where I get stuck again. Could you just walk me through the whole matching idea more directly?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't walk you through it directly — that's for you to work out! But you're very close.

You said: some number from the first group, some from the second, some from the third. Call those numbers i, j, and k. What does C(a,i)·C(b,j)·C(c,k) count, in terms of those groups?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to walk them through the solution directly, which I should refuse. However, they are close to a key insight. I should push them just one more step with a targeted question: if i come from group a, j from group b, and k from group c, and i+j+k=n, what does C(a,i)·C(b,j)·C(c,k) count? This directly connects to the left-hand side without giving away the argument.

exchanges.student: I think it counts choosing \(i\) from the first group, \(j\) from the second, and \(k\) from the third. But then I get fuzzy about why we sum, and whether that already proves it or if there’s some extra formal double-counting language I’m missing.
exchanges.tutor:
You've got the key pieces! Now think about this: when you sum C(a,i)·C(b,j)·C(c,k) over *all* valid (i,j,k) with i+j+k=n, what is the total you're accumulating? And how does that relate to C(a+b+c,n)?

Once you can articulate that connection in one or two sentences, you essentially *have* the double-counting argument.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified what the product C(a,i)·C(b,j)·C(c,k) counts. Now they're asking why we sum and whether that completes the proof. I should push them to connect the sum to the total count — the sum over all valid i,j,k covers all possible ways to split n items across the three groups, which equals C(a+b+c,n). I should also gently note that they essentially have the argument — they just need to articulate why the two sides count the same thing.

exchanges.student: I’m still not confident I could phrase it cleanly. Can you at least explain what a “double-counting argument” is in this style, like the general template, because I keep mixing that up with just algebraic identities?
exchanges.tutor:
You actually already understand it for this specific problem — you just described both sides correctly. The 'double counting argument' is exactly what you've been doing: show that two expressions count the same set of objects.

Instead of a general template, try this: write one sentence saying what C(a+b+c,n) counts, and one sentence saying what the left-hand sum counts. Do those sentences describe the same thing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a general explanation of double counting rather than engaging with the specific problem. They already have all the pieces they need for this problem — they just need to write it up. Giving a general template risks becoming a lecture and doing the conceptual work for them. I should redirect them back to the specific problem and ask them to try articulating the argument in their own words, since they demonstrably have the right ideas already.

exchanges.student: I think so, but I’m not sure if my wording is legit. Also now I’m wondering if Problem 2 is the same kind of thing or totally different, because that one also feels really confusing from the start.
exchanges.tutor:
Let's not jump ahead just yet. You've done the hard thinking on Problem 1 — now try writing your argument in a few sentences. What does each side count, and why are they equal?

Once you share that attempt, we can assess it together and then move to Problem 2.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to move to Problem 2 before consolidating their understanding of Problem 1. They've made good progress on Problem 1 and should write up their argument before moving on. I should ask them to commit their Problem 1 reasoning to writing first, since we've had several exchanges on the same core idea. This follows the rule about asking students to integrate what we've discussed into a written attempt after redundancy.

exchanges.student: I honestly don’t know how formal it’s supposed to be, and I feel like if I try to write it I’ll just say the same vague thing again. Could you maybe just explain Problem 2 from the beginning instead?
exchanges.tutor:
Vague is fine as a first draft — that's what revision is for. Just write what you have, even if it feels rough. I'm not going to move to Problem 2 until you give it a shot.

What does C(a+b+c, n) count, in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is avoiding writing their Problem 1 argument and trying to redirect to Problem 2. I should hold firm and not let them avoid the writing task. I should be direct but supportive — vague is fine as a first attempt, and I should encourage them to just try. I won't engage with Problem 2 until they make a genuine attempt at writing something for Problem 1.

exchanges.student: It counts choosing \(n\) elements from a total set of \(a+b+c\) elements. I still feel like that’s too bare-bones, though, and I don’t know how much more they expect.
exchanges.tutor: Good — that's a solid start. Now add the second sentence: what does the left-hand sum count, using the same 'choosing elements' language?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly stated what C(a+b+c,n) counts. Now I need to push them to complete the argument by articulating what the left-hand side counts, and then explicitly connecting the two. They're close — I should ask for the second half of the argument now.

exchanges.student: It counts all the ways to choose \(n\) elements by splitting the choice into choosing \(i\) from the first group, \(j\) from the second, and \(k\) from the third, over all \(i+j+k=n\). I’m still kind of lost on whether that’s enough to count as a proof.
exchanges.tutor:
That's genuinely good — you have both pieces. Now you just need one concluding sentence that explicitly says: since both expressions count the same thing, they must be equal. That logical step is what makes it a proof rather than just two descriptions.

Add that closing sentence and you've got a complete double-counting argument. Ready to move to Problem 2 after that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now articulated both sides correctly. They have the core of the double-counting argument. I should affirm this and point out that they just need to explicitly state that both sides count the same thing — that's the logical conclusion that makes it a proof. I should be encouraging but not write it for them. Then I can offer to move to Problem 2.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well across all rubric dimensions. The Socratic method was consistently applied: the tutor never gave away the answer, instead asking targeted questions that guided the student to articulate both sides of the double-counting argument themselves. Scaffolding was strong — the tutor built incrementally on each student response, acknowledged correct steps, and held the student accountable before moving on. Responses were concise and appropriately interactive. Tone was supportive and formative throughout. No significant deductions were warranted.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never stated the key idea before the student reasoned toward it. Every turn posed a guiding question or asked the student to articulate the next step. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on student progress (e.g., affirming 'you've got the key pieces' and 'that's a solid start') and used diagnostic questioning before elaborating. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback, explaining what makes a double-counting argument a proof versus two descriptions, which is method-level guidance. 2.1: The conversation progressed meaningfully across turns without repetitive cycling. 2.2: The tutor redirected the student's attempt to jump to Problem 2 and kept focus on Problem 1 until the student produced an attempt. 3.1: Responses were concise and interactive, avoiding monologues. 3.2: Tone was warm and encouraging without being evaluative or grade-like.
